result:
The old man at the laundromat told me that time doesn't move. He was folding a shirt that was too large for him, careful along the seams the way my mother used to be, and he said it like he was reporting the weather. Time doesn't move. We do. We tumble through it like sheets in a dryer, spinning and tumbling and coming out softer than we went in, whether we want to or not.

I didn't argue with him. I was twenty-three and thought about arguing with everyone back then, thought the highest form of dignity was refusing to let a statement stand if I disagreed with it. But something about the way he said it—flat, the way you say things you've tested and found true—made me hold my tongue. I let it sit. It's still sitting, more than a decade later, gathering dust and meaning in equal measure.

I think about him when I walk at night. I've always been a night walker. Something about the dark makes the world feel permission-giving. In daylight, you are supposed to be going somewhere. At night, wandering is the point. The streets empty out and you can hear your own footsteps and the destination ceases to matter and you're just a body moving through cool air, present in a way that the urgent daylight never allows.

My grandmother walked at night too, though she'd never have admitted it. I'd catch her sometimes on visits, standing at the kitchen window at two in the morning, looking out at the yard. When I asked what she was doing, she'd say, "Listening." I'd stand next to her and listen and hear nothing. Crickets, maybe. The hum of the refrigerator. I told her I didn't hear anything. She said, "Exactly."

I didn't understand her then. I was a teenager, and silence was just the absence of something I wanted—music, conversation, the noise of being alive and noticed. Silence felt like waiting. Now I'm thirty-six and I understand her completely. Silence isn't absence. It's the thing underneath. It's what's there when you stop performing. My grandmother was standing at that window shedding the day, letting the performance drop, and listening to whatever was underneath it all. I think it was probably love, though she'd never have said that word. Too big for her mouth. She kept big things in small gestures. The way she'd save the best piece of chicken and put it on your plate like it was an accident. The way she'd iron your socks.

The laundromat man, my grandmother—I've been collecting them over the years, these people who handed me something I wasn't ready for. A third-grade teacher who told me that being smart wasn't the same as being curious and that the latter would serve me better. A stranger on a bus in a city I was just passing through who looked at me and said, "You look like someone who's about to make a decision," and I was, I was, and I didn't know how she saw it. Maybe we wear our turning points more visibly than we think.

I wonder sometimes if the whole project of being human is just this: receiving things we're not ready for, carrying them confused and heavy, and then one day—years later, usually, in the shower or reaching for a coffee cup—suddenly understanding what we were given. The delayed gift. The letter that arrives decades after it was sent.

My daughter is four now. She asks questions that have the weight of philosophy wrapped in the syntax of simplicity. "Where was I before I was me?" she asked last week. I almost said "nowhere," which would have been the easy answer, the clean answer, the one that closes the door. But instead I said, "I think you were everywhere." She considered this, nodded once, and went back to her drawing. A purple sun over a blue house. She'd drawn the door too small for anyone to enter through, but the windows were enormous. I didn't correct her. I want her to grow up in a world where the windows are bigger than the doors. Where looking out matters more than getting in.

Maybe that's what the laundromat man meant. We're always arriving at moments we've already lived through, understanding them for the first time. Time isn't a river we float down. It's a house we walk through repeatedly, seeing new rooms each time, wondering how we missed them before. The rooms were always there. We just weren't ready to see them.

I fold my own shirts carefully now, along the seams. I listen to silence. I walk at night and call it prayer. And when my daughter asks her big questions, I try to give her answers that are doors, not walls—answers that stay open, that she can walk through again later and find something new on the other side.

We tumble. We soften. We arrive, again and again, at the same moment, finally ready for it.

Time doesn't move.

We do.
